refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication latency with p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margins. The p95 percentile metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions. Refinement clarifies that this is an end-to-end requirement spanning sensor acquisition, network transmission, and command execution.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure round-trip latency from operator input (e.g., joystick command) through network to equipment response (e.g., actuator movement) under representative network conditions. Collect statistical distribution and confirm p95 ≤ 150 ms across 1000+ samples under load.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Sensor data acquisition and local processing shall introduce ≤ 30 ms latency
FR-NGHE-008.2: Network transmission (uplink and downlink) shall introduce ≤ 80 ms latency under agreed conditions
FR-NGHE-008.3: Remote command processing and actuator response shall introduce ≤ 40 ms latency

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement low-latency teleoperation architecture with edge processing, optimized network codec, and direct command-to-actuator pathways
DP-B: Deploy dedicated low-latency network overlay (e.g., MPLS, SD-WAN) with QoS prioritization for teleoperation traffic
DP-C: Hybrid approach combining edge processing, network optimization, and adaptive codec selection based on network conditions
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid approach combining edge processing (local sensor fusion and command validation), network optimization (QoS-prioritized teleoperation channel with adaptive codec), and adaptive latency compensation (predictive control feedback) to achieve p95 ≤ 150 ms under variable network conditions.

potential_sources: Coupling between sensor acquisition timing and network transmission scheduling (mitigated by buffering strategy)
Coupling between codec selection and network bandwidth availability (mitigated by adaptive algorithm)
Coupling between command validation logic and actuator control firmware (mitigated by standardized command interface)
coupling_check.notes: DP-C exhibits acceptable decoupling. Edge processing is independent of network layer; network optimization is independent of actuator firmware. Adaptive codec selection creates weak coupling but is manageable through well-defined thresholds and fallback modes.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network conditions remain within agreed parameters (latency floor, jitter bounds, packet loss < 1%)
Assumption: Edge processing hardware has sufficient compute capacity for real-time sensor fusion
Risk: Unpredictable network spikes could exceed p95 target; mitigation: implement latency monitoring and operator alerts
Risk: Codec adaptation overhead could introduce additional latency; mitigation: pre-compute codec parameters and use hardware acceleration
Risk: Predictive control feedback may diverge from actual equipment state under extreme conditions; mitigation: periodic state synchronization
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify network conditions baseline (latency, jitter, packet loss percentiles) and define fallback behavior if p95 target is breached (e.g., operator alert, reduced autonomy mode, graceful degradation).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all teleoperation-specific logic including operator command reception, network transmission/reception, remote command processing, latency monitoring, and adaptive codec management. Owns the end-to-end latency budget and coordinates with Vehicle for actuator control and Safety Services for safety validation.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (joystick input, mode selection) from tele-operator interface; carries command payload with timestamp for latency tracking

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits sensor data (position, orientation, load, diagnostics) to tele-operator display; includes acquisition timestamp for latency calculation

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional network communication channel; Teleoperation module manages QoS prioritization, codec selection, and latency monitoring on this interface

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and alerts from Safety Services; may trigger command throttling or latency-aware mode transitions

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained to own teleoperation-specific latency logic. Teleoperation would be a major subsystem within NGHE Solution, not contained by it at this decomposition level.

Vehicle module owns actuator control and local sensor acquisition but does not own network communication or tele-operator interface logic. Teleoperation latency spans beyond vehicle boundaries (includes network and operator interface).

Teleoperation module is purpose-built for this requirement. It owns operator command reception, network transmission, remote command processing, and latency monitoring. Minimal diffusion: only interfaces to Vehicle (for actuator commands) and Safety Services (for constraints) are external.
Safety Services owns safety validation and alerts but does not own teleoperation-specific latency optimization. Latency is a safety enabler, not a safety service itself.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP-C because it encapsulates all three pillars of the hybrid approach: (1) edge processing coordination (sensor fusion timing), (2) network optimization (QoS, codec adaptation), and (3) latency compensation (predictive feedback). The module has clear boundaries: it receives Tasking commands, transmits Telemetry, manages Network Link, and respects SafetyAlert constraints.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle module owns sensor acquisition timing, Teleoperation may not have full control over sensor-to-network latency; mitigation: define sensor data ready signal as part of Telemetry interface contract

Risk: If Safety Services enforces hard latency limits, Teleoperation may need to implement mode transitions; mitigation: SafetyAlert interface should include latency thresholds and fallback mode guidance
Risk: Network Link interface may be shared with other modules (e.g., diagnostics, firmware updates); mitigation: implement QoS prioritization and bandwidth reservation for teleoperation traffic
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define the Telemetry interface contract to include sensor acquisition timestamp and readiness signal. Define SafetyAlert interface to include latency thresholds and mode transition guidance. Establish Network Link QoS policy to reserve bandwidth and prioritize teleoperation traffic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid approach) directly addresses the refined FR by combining edge processing (reduces local latency), network optimization (reduces transmission latency), and adaptive compensation (handles variable conditions). The DP is traceable to all sub-FRs and includes explicit latency budgeting. No functional gaps identified.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module depends on Vehicle module for actuator response timing; this is acceptable as a controlled external dependency with clear interface contract
Teleoperation module depends on Safety Services for constraint validation; this is acceptable as a safety-critical dependency with well-defined SafetyAlert interface
Network Link interface is shared with other potential consumers; mitigation: implement QoS policy to prevent latency degradation from competing traffic
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score with minimal diffusion (0.05). The module owns all teleoperation-specific logic and coordinates cleanly with Vehicle and Safety Services via well-defined interfaces. No architectural diffusion concerns identified.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via end-to-end latency measurement under controlled network conditions. Teleoperation module can instrument Tasking input timestamp and Telemetry output timestamp to calculate round-trip latency. Statistical analysis (p95 percentile) is straightforward.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns the latency budget and is responsible for achieving p95 ≤ 150 ms. Sub-FR allocation to sub-modules (edge processing, network optimization, latency compensation) is deferred to next decomposition level but is architecturally sound.

recommended_revisions: Define Telemetry interface contract to include sensor acquisition timestamp for latency tracking
Define SafetyAlert interface to include latency thresholds and mode transition guidance
Establish Network Link QoS policy to reserve bandwidth and prioritize teleoperation traffic
Document fallback behavior if p95 target is breached (e.g., operator alert, graceful degradation)

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-formed and traceable to DP-C, which is architecturally sound and assigned to the Teleoperation module with high containment (0.95) and low diffusion (0.05). All gate reviews pass. However, the FR is not atomic: it decomposes into three sub-FRs (sensor acquisition latency, network transmission latency, actuator response latency) that require further refinement and DP selection. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to allocate latency budgets to sub-modules and define detailed design parameters for edge processing, network optimization, and latency compensation.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Sensor data acquisition and local processing shall introduce ≤ 30 ms latency
FR-NGHE-008.2: Network transmission (uplink and downlink) shall introduce ≤ 80 ms latency under agreed conditions
FR-NGHE-008.3: Remote command processing and actuator response shall introduce ≤ 40 ms latency

assumptions: Network conditions remain within agreed parameters (latency floor, jitter bounds, packet loss < 1%)
Edge processing hardware has sufficient compute capacity for real-time sensor fusion
Vehicle module provides sensor data with predictable timing and Telemetry interface includes acquisition timestamp
Safety Services enforces safety constraints via SafetyAlert interface without introducing additional latency
Network Link interface supports QoS prioritization and bandwidth reservation
open_risks: Unpredictable network spikes could exceed p95 target; mitigation: implement latency monitoring and operator alerts
Codec adaptation overhead could introduce additional latency; mitigation: pre-compute codec parameters and use hardware acceleration
Predictive control feedback may diverge from actual equipment state under extreme conditions; mitigation: periodic state synchronization
Network Link interface may be shared with other modules, causing latency degradation; mitigation: implement QoS policy
evidence_needed: Network baseline characterization (latency, jitter, packet loss percentiles under agreed conditions)
Edge processing hardware performance specifications (compute capacity, sensor fusion algorithm latency)
Codec performance data (latency vs. bandwidth trade-offs for candidate codecs)
Vehicle module sensor acquisition timing and Telemetry interface contract
Safety Services latency thresholds and mode transition guidance
Network Link QoS policy and bandwidth reservation mechanism
